title: The Docket - Week 9
subject: The 'AI lawyer' app just got someone arrested. We need to talk about unauthorized practice.
preheader: Plus: the open-source model that outperforms paid legal AI tools
bodyHtml:
The Docket

LegalTech AI Hub · Week of June 10, 2026

The line between “legal information” and “legal advice” just got a criminal case study. Here’s your week.

🚨 AI Legal App Founder Charged With Unauthorized Practice of Law

The founder of “LawyerBot,” a consumer-facing AI app that provided legal guidance on immigration matters, has been criminally charged with unauthorized practice of law in Florida. The app didn’t just provide information — it told users which specific visa to apply for, drafted supporting letters, and advised them on interview strategy. A user followed the app’s advice, applied for the wrong visa category, and was detained at the border. This is the first criminal UPL case involving an AI product. It won’t be the last.

💻 Open-Source Legal Model Outperforms Commercial Tools

LegalLlama, an open-source model fine-tuned on publicly available legal texts, outperformed three leading commercial legal AI tools on the LegalBench benchmark. It’s free, runs locally (no data leaves your machine), and doesn’t require a per-seat license. For firms concerned about sending client data to third-party APIs, this is a game-changer. The catch: you need technical staff to deploy it. See our full comparison.

🇬🇧 UK Solicitors Regulation Authority Publishes AI Guidance

The SRA published detailed guidance on AI use by solicitors, including specific examples of acceptable and unacceptable uses. Most notable: using AI for initial research is fine; using AI to generate client advice without lawyer review is a professional conduct violation. They also clarified that firms are responsible for AI errors to the same extent as human errors — no “the computer did it” defense.

Tool Spotlight

Argument Gap Analyzer — Now With Counterargument Suggestions

We’ve updated the Argument Gap Analyzer with a new feature: for each gap it identifies, it now suggests specific counterarguments you should prepare for. Think of it as your opposing counsel’s brain, available for free before you file.

Try It Free →

The UPL story is the one to watch. How we draw the line between AI-as-tool and AI-as-lawyer will define the next chapter of legal technology. Reply with your take.
